questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Acetylene can be obtained by the reaction?
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mover accent="false"><mo>→</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow></mover></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><munder><mrow><mover accent="false"><mo>→</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>.</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>443</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow></munder></math>
questions.solution:
Iodoform and Silver (Ag) undergo dehalogenation reaction to produce acetylene.

(A) HCOOK+H2O→∆H2+CO2+KOH

(B) 2CHI3Iodoform+6AgSilver→∆CH≡CHAcetylene+6AgI

(C) CH3CH2OH→Conc.H2SO4  443KCH2=CH2ethylene+H2O

(D) Be2C+H2O→CH4Methane
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The major product of the following reaction is
questions.solution: The given reaction is an example of Wolff-Kishner reduction. Wolff-Kishner reduction mechanism begins with the formation of a hydrazone anion which then releases the nitrogen molecule to form a carbanion. This carbanion then reacts with the water in the system to give a hydrocarbon. Typically, diethylene glycol is used as a solvent for this method.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q:
The major product formed in the following reaction sequence is

CH3CH=CH2 →iii CH3CH2CHOiv CrO3i HBr, peroxideii Mg
questions.solution:
Propene undergoes anti-markonikov's addtion with HBr in presence of peroxide to from 1-bromopropane.

1-bromopropane reacts with magnesium in presence of dry ether to form the grignard reagent propylmagnesium bromide.

Propanal undergoes nucleophilic addition with propylmagnesium bromide to form hexan-3-ol which on oxidation with CrO3 froms hexan-3-one

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The correct order of rates of addition of Br2/water to the following alkenes is

[A] CH2=CH2

[B] CH2=CH-NO2

[C] CH3-CH2-CH=CH2

[D] CH3-CHCH3|=CH2
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></mfenced></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Addition of Br2/ water to the alkene is example of electrophilic addition reaction. This reaction take place in two steps. In first step electrophile is added to the alkene and intermediate carbocation is formed. This step is also called rate determining step. 

So, reactivity of this reaction depends on stability of carbocation formed and as we know for carbocation 3° > 2° >1°. So, reactivity order will be

[B] CH2=CH-NO2 < [A] CH2=CH2 < [C] CH3-CH2-CH=CH2 < [D] CH3-CHCH3|=CH2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
In which of the following reactions, $t$-butyl benzene is formed
1. Benzene $+t$-butyl chloride $\stackrel{\mathrm{AlCl}_3}{\longrightarrow}$
2. Benzene $+\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_2 \stackrel{\mathrm{BF}_3}{\stackrel{\mathrm{HF}}{\longrightarrow}}$
3. Benzene $+t$-butyl alcohol $\stackrel{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}{\longrightarrow}$
4. Benzene + butanoyl chloride $\underset{\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{HCl}}{\stackrel{\mathrm{AlCl}_3}{\longrightarrow}}$
questions.solution:
The given reactions take place as follows. 1. The replacement of H-atom in benzene, by alkyl group can be brought about alkyl chloride in presence of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$. Reaction is known as Friedel-Craft's alkylation reaction.
2. In this reaction, benzene reacts to 2 -methyl propane, produce $t$-butyl benzene.
3. This can also be carried out by using alkanol and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$.
4. The reaction I, II and III gives $t$-butyl benzene, while IV gives phenyl propanone.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Among the following reactions, the reaction that "does not" give alkane product is
questions.solution:
According to the question, without option (d) other methods are used in preparation of alkanes. 

Option (a) belong to reduction of alkyne, due to this alkene is formed and further reduction of alkene, alkane is formed (Sebatier and senderens reaction). 

The given reaction in option (b) is known as kolbe's electrolytic method. During this reaction butane is formed. 

The given reaction in option (c) is the example of wurtz reaction, This reaction is taken place between alkyl halide and metallic sodium in the presence of dry ether.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Benzene reacts with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math>-propyl chloride in the presence of anhydrous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AlCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> to give predominantly
questions.solution:
This reaction is known as Friedel Craft's alkylation reaction. in presence of anhydrous AlCl3, n-propyl chloride introduce an alkylating agent. It is an electrophilic substitution reaction. 

Reaction occurs in following step.

Generation of electrophile.

CH3CH2CH2Cl+AlCl3→CH3CH2CH2++AlCl4-

Carbocation formed in step 1 rearranges to form secondary carbocation.

CH3CH2CH2+↔ CH3CH+CH3      1°                    2°

This secondary carbocation attack on benzene ring.

Next step is the abstraction of proton from conjugate base

Hence, the product is isopropyl benzene.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: An alkene $A\left(\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_8\right)$ exhibits cis/trans isomerism. $A$ on ozonolysis gives $B$, which when reacted with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ followed by hydroxylamine gave $C$. What are $B$ and $C$ ?
questions.solution: Here, $(B)$ is $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}$ and $(C)$ is $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}==\mathrm{CHCH}=\mathrm{NOH}$. (i) On ozonolysis double bond get broken to give $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}$. (ii) On reaction with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ followed by hydroxyl amine, we get $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}=\mathrm{NOH}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q:
Which of the following reactions are not feasible?
I. $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 \frac{\mathrm{HCl}}{\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CO}\right)_2 \mathrm{O}_2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}$
II. $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl} \stackrel{\mathrm{AlCl}_3}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2$
III. $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHClCH}_2 \mathrm{Cl} \underset{\Delta}{\stackrel{\mathrm{KOH}}{\longrightarrow}} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{C} \equiv \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{CH}}$
IV. $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_3 \stackrel{\mathrm{KMnO}_4 / \mathrm{H}^{+}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}$
questions.solution: $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2$ to $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{HCl}$ in a peroxide is not feasible as peroxide/kharash effect is not observed for $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{HI}$. feasible as this reaction would give an alkene which when treated with sodamíde gives alkyne. Thus, I and III are not feasible.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

Z is a cumulated diene system
Y is antiaromatic system
Hence stability order is X >W >Z >Y

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Sodium acetate was electrolysed by Kolbe's method to form two gases \(A\) and \(B\) at anode. \(C\) and \(D\) are formed, when \(B\) is heated with regulated supply of \(\mathrm{O}_2\) or air in the presence of \(\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}\right)_2 \mathrm{Mn}\). \(\mathrm{C}\) reacts with \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) to form a salt. \(A\) and \(D\) are respectively,
questions.solution:
Kolbe electrolysis of \(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}\). At anode,
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CH}_3^*+\mathrm{CH}_3^* \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \underset{(\mathrm{A})}{-\mathrm{CH}_3} \\ & 2 \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_3+7 \mathrm{O}_2 \stackrel{\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}_2 \mathrm{Mn}\right.}{\longrightarrow} 4 \mathrm{CO}_2+\underset{\mathrm{(D)}}{6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}} \end{aligned}\) \(\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \underset{\mathrm{(C)}}{\mathrm{CO}}+\mathrm{O}_3\) \(\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text{(Salt)}}{\mathrm{HCOONa}}\) Hence, \(A\) is \(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6\) and \(D\) is \(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: Which of the following reactions will not give pure propane?
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="false"><mo>→</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow></mover></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mover accent="false"><mo>→</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></mover></math>
questions.solution:
Here in option A reaction will not give pure propane
CH3CH2Cl+2Na+ClCH2CH3→dryetherCH3CH2CH2CH3+2NaCl
CH3Cl+2Na+ClCH3→dryetherCH3CH3+2NaCl
CH3CH2Cl+2Na+ClCH3→dryetherCH3CH2CH3+NaCl
Thus, we get a mixture containing butane, ethane and propane.
In rest other reaction pure propane is possible.

questions.q: The product formed when a hydrocarbon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>'</mo></math> of molecular formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>10</mn></msub></math> is reacted with sodamide is subjected to ozonolysis, followed by hydrolysis with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Zn</mi><mo>/</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, and upon further oxidation gave two carboxylic acids, of which one is optically active. The hydrocarbon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>'</mo></math>' is
questions.solution:
The molecule is reacting with sodamide, therefore, it should be terminal alkyne. Now, this alkyne is subjected ozonolysis followed by oxidation giving two carboxylic acids in which one carboxylic acid is optically active among them. Hence, the alkyne should be:3−Methylpent−1−yne.

The reaction is shown below: CH3CH2CHCH3C≡CH→Zn/H2O2O3CH3CH2CHCH3COOHOptically active+HCOOH

questions.q: Which of the following alkenes will yield 2-methyl propanal on reductive ozonolysis. (addition with ozone followed by the reaction with \(\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\) ).
questions.solution:
2,5 dimethyl hex -3-ene on reductive ozonolysis in presence of \(\mathrm{O}_3\) and \(\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\), gives 2 moles of 2-methyl propanal.
Hence, the correct option is (c).
